raw_case:
Case Report: Medial Clavicle Physeal Injury Mimicking a Posterior Clavicular Dislocation in a Young Adult


Abstract



Introduction:

Sternoclavicular injuries are rare and account for 1 % of all dislocations. Medial clavicular physeal separation is another rare entity and may sometimes mimic a sternoclavicular dislocation. Diagnosis is usually missed in young adults due to lack of clinical suspicion.

Case Report:

A 22-year-old male patient sustained a right shoulder girdle injury due to a motorcycle accident. He was clinically diagnosed as a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation. On further imaging a medial clavicular physeal injury was suspected. An attempt of closed reduction was attempted but not successful so it was converted to an open reduction. Physeal injury was confirmed and reduction was fixed with transosseous sutures using low cost polyglactin suture material. Patient had a good clinical recovery.

Conclusion:

Medial clavicle physeal injury may mimic sternoclavicular dislocations and should be clinically suspected in young adult patients.

Learning Point of the Article:

Medial clavicle physeal injuries are rare and may present as a dislocation of the sternoclavicular joint which may lead to a diagnostic dilemma for the orthopedic surgeon.

Introduction

Roughly 1% of all dislocations and 1-3% of all shoulder girdle injuries are caused by sternoclavicular joint injuries, which are uncommon clinical entities[1]. Another rare lesion is the medial physeal fracture separation of the clavicle which usually occurs in young adults and adolescents. There is a partial or complete separation between the clavicle and its epiphysis and mimics a sternoclavicular separation. 50 percent of pediatric patients undergoing open reduction and internal fixation for posteriorly displaced sternoclavicular joint injuries had a fracture through the medial clavicle physis, according to a research [2].Medial clavicular physeal separation can occur till the fusion of the medial clavicular physis takes place which is usually between 22 to 25 years of age because of its late appearance and subsequently late fusion[3]. Epiphyseal injury is usually not suspected in young adults and so the diagnosis between a sternoclavicular dislocation and a medial clavicle physeal injury becomes a clinical dilemma.We present a case of a 22-year-old male you sustained a bike accident and presented to us with right side sternoclavicular pain. He was suspected as having a posterior dislocation of the medial clavicle. CT images raised suspicions of a physeal injury which was confirmed intraoperatively.

Case Report

A 22-year-old male patient presented in our OPD with pain in the right sternoclavicular region which aggravated on overhead activities. He sustained a fall on the right outstretched hand during a bike accident eight hours prior to presenting to us. On clinical examination there was tenderness over the medial sternoclavicular joint as well as posterior dip in the medial clavicle (Fig. 1). This led us too clinically suspect a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation. The radiographs appeared normal (Fig. 2) so we advised a 3D reconstruction CT imaging of the right clavicle and sternum. CT images were suggestive of a posterior dislocation of the medial end of the clavicle (Fig 3). However, one section of the CT appeared to show a flake of bone attached to the sternum. Medial clavicular physeal injury was a secondary diagnosis which was kept in consideration. We decided to take the patient up for closed reduction under anesthesia and covert to open reduction if closed attempts failed.Once the patient was sedated, we tried a closed reduction of the medial end of the clavicle however it failed. So, we decide to go ahead with open reduction of the medial end of the clavicle over the sternum. An incision was taken over the medial aspect of clavicle and the sternoclavicular joint (Fig. 4). Once we reached the sternoclavicular joint capsule, we realized that the medial clavicular physis was still attached to the capsule (Fig. 5) and that there had a been a physeal separation injury and the clavicle went posteriorly and got locked thus mimicking a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation. The reduction was done by giving lateral traction to the distal fragment using a towel clip (Fig. 6). Once reduction was confirmed we proceeded with fixing both the fragments using polyglactin No 1 braided absorbable suture material. Two transosseous sutures were taken through the distal clavicle and the medial clavicular epiphysis, once each at the inferior and the superior end (Fig. 7). The fixation was stable and the distal fragment was no longer dislocating posteriorly (Fig. 8). The sternoclavicular joint capsule was repaired (Fig. 9) and after thoroughly giving a normal saline wound wash the incision was closed in layers.The patient was put in an arm sling for 4 weeks and was advised strict immobilization on the shoulder girdle. At 4 weeks passive range of motion exercises were initiated along with shoulder girdle stabilization exercises and at 6 weeks active range of motion exercises. The patient regained full active range of motion by 12 weeks and was then started on girdle strengthening exercises. At 6 months post-surgery the patient had regained preinjury levels of strength.

Discussion

Falling or engaging in sports-related activities can result in a direct hit to the shoulder, which is the most common cause of sternoclavicular joint injuries. They are common in the 4th decade and have a slight male predilection [1]. Epidemiological studies have shown that medial clavicular injuries are the least frequent (2% of all clavicular injuries) while road traffic accidents remains the most common cause of these injury patterns [4]. Medial clavicular physis takes place which is usually between 22 to 25 years of age because of its late appearance and subsequently late fusion[3]. As the sternoclavicular joint and the medial clavicular physis are close to each other it becomes difficult to clinically distinguish a true posterior sternoclavicular dislocation from a medial clavicular physeal injury in such cases[5]. A high index of clinical suspicion should be kept after a high energy traumatic injury to the shoulder girdle in adolescents and young adults[6]. Since our patient was a young adult with a shoulder girdle high impact injury, we had high level of suspicion for this injury presentation intraoperatively.Standard radiographs usually appear normal and maybe misleading. Therefore, it is imperative that we perform a CT scan in such patients[7]. In order to determine the precise location of the fragment and plan reduction as well as osteosynthesis, a CT scan can be used to evaluate the relationship between the clavicular shaft and physis and the major vessels that are just posterior to it[8]. In our case the radiographs essentially appeared normal. It was only on CT images that we could identify a posteriorly displaced clavicular shaft with a small medial epiphyseal bone.Mekkaoui et al in their case report described a similar injury in a 16-year-old boy due to a motorcycle accident. They also suspected a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation which eventually turned out to be a medial clavicle physeal injury. They treatment with opened reduction and fixation with a k wire. The k wire was removed at 6 weeks and a similar rehab protocol as ours was followed. The patient had excellent clinical recovery[6]. Beckmann et al in his report of an adolescent female basketball player described a similar injury. They were unable to diagnose the case on radiographs as well as a CT scan. They additionally concluded that MRI is a good imaging option if such injuries cannot be detected on radiographs and CT. Similar to our case a closed reduction trial was given and it failed so the reduction was done under vison and fixed with a trans osseous fiber wire sutures in a figure of eight pattern which was similar to our case[9]. In our case we used a polyglactin suture instead of a fiber wire and still achieved good functional outcome. The advantage of a polyglactin suture is that it is cost effective as well as absorbable in nature unlike fiberwires. It retains its strength for 4 weeks and is completely absorbed by 12 weeks which is enough for clinical recovery in such cases[10].More sternoclavicular dislocations occur anteriorly than posteriorly, with posterior dislocations accounting for as much as 25–33% of all sternoclavicular dislocations[1]. Posterior dislocations have been reported to injure mediastinal tissues such the trachea, brachial plexus, and major vessels. It has been found that 25–50% of patients with sternoclavicular joint posterior dislocations also present with clinical evidence of mediastinal compression[11]. The majority of posterior sternoclavicular injuries will require open reduction and fixation, despite certain studies recommending attempted close reduction for all of them as it may reduce up to 40–50% of these injuries[1,11,12].It is possible for sternoclavicular injuries to manifest as a physeal fracture or as a capsular injury until the physis fuses. Physeal fracture-dislocations of the sternoclavicular joint, as opposed to a pure posterior dislocation of the joint, were seen in 50% of the 40 skeletally immature patients with posterior sternoclavicular injuries who had open reduction and internal fixation, according to a research by Lee et al. [2].

Conclusion

Medial clavicle physeal injury is a rarely documented injury pattern especially in young adults. However, it should be kept in mind as a clinical diagnosis for young adults presenting with medial clavicular injuries as this physis fuses by 25 years of age. Most of these cases get misdiagnosed as sternoclavicular dislocations as clinical suspicion of a physeal injury is very limited among orthopaedic surgeons in these age group. Additional investigations like a CT are essential in these cases and minimum attempts at closed reduction should be attempted due to proximity of mediastinal structures. Trans osseous fixation with low-cost absorbable sutures have good functional outcomes post operatively.

Clinical Message

Medial clavicular physeal maybe misdiagnosed as a sternoclavicular dislocation in the adolescent or young adult age group due to low clinical suspicion. Open reduction and transosseous fixation give excellent results in such cases.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 22-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Pain in the right sternoclavicular region, aggravated by overhead activities.
- **History of Present Illness:** Sustained a fall on his right outstretched hand in a motorcycle accident, presenting 8 hours post-injury.
- **Past Medical History:** None mentioned.
- **Personal History:** None mentioned.
- **Family History:** None mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** 
  - Tenderness over the medial sternoclavicular joint.
  - Posterior dip in the medial clavicle, clinically suggesting a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Radiographs appeared normal.
  - 3D CT scan suggested a posterior dislocation of the medial clavicle with a flake of bone attached to the sternum, raising suspicion of a medial clavicular physeal injury.
  - Diagnosis of medial clavicular physeal injury confirmed intraoperatively after initial failure of closed reduction.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Diagnosis: Posterior Sternoclavicular Dislocation**
   - Clinical examination (posterior dip and tenderness) supported this diagnosis.
   - Confirmed by CT scan showing posterior displacement of the medial clavicle.
   - However, one CT section raised suspicion of a physeal injury, which prompted further consideration.

2. **Revised Diagnosis: Medial Clavicular Physeal Injury**
   - Considered due to the patient’s age (22 years, within the range for physeal fusion) and CT findings of a small epiphyseal bone.
   - Differentiated intraoperatively when the physeal separation with posterior locking of the clavicle was identified. 

3. **Exclusion of Pure Dislocation Without Physeal Injury**
   - Intraoperative findings confirmed a physeal separation rather than a simple posterior sternoclavicular dislocation.

**Rationale for Exclusion:** 
- The clinical presentation and imaging findings alone could not differentiate between the two conditions.
- The intraoperative findings of a physeal separation confirmed the diagnosis.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Medial clavicular physeal separation mimicking posterior sternoclavicular dislocation. Diagnosis confirmed intraoperatively, supported by imaging and clinical suspicion based on patient age and mechanism of injury.

---

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 22-year-old male with right sternoclavicular pain after a motorcycle accident where he fell on his outstretched right hand. The main complaint is pain in that area, worse with overhead activities. 

First, I need to think about the anatomy here. The sternoclavicular joint (SC joint) is a saddle-type joint connecting the clavicle to the sternum. It's pretty stable due to strong ligaments, so dislocations here are rare but possible. Since he fell on an outstretched hand, that force could transmit up the arm to the clavicle.

His physical exam shows tenderness over the medial SC joint and a posterior dip, which clinically suggests a posterior dislocation. Posterior dislocations are less common than anterior but more concerning because they can compress nearby structures like the trachea, esophagus, or major vessels. But in this case, there's no mention of breathing issues or other symptoms, so maybe it's not causing severe compression.

The radiographs were normal. Wait, that's interesting. SC joint injuries can be hard to see on standard X-rays because of overlapping structures. So a CT scan would be more detailed. The 3D CT showed a posterior dislocation with a bone flake attached to the sternum. The mention of a physeal injury is key here. Wait, the patient is 22. The medial clavicular physis typically closes around 23-25 years old. So even at 22, he might still have an open growth plate. That makes a physeal injury (Salter-Harris type) possible, which could mimic a dislocation. 

Physeal injuries in this area might not be easily distinguished from true dislocations, especially since the epiphysis may not be ossified fully. The CT showing a bone flake attached to the sternum suggests an avulsion fracture, which could be part of a physeal separation. So the initial diagnosis of posterior SC dislocation might actually be a physeal fracture-dislocation. 

They tried closed reduction first, which failed, leading to surgery. Intraoperatively, they confirmed a medial clavicular physeal injury. So the final diagnosis is a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation due to a physeal injury, which in this case is a fracture through the growth plate rather than a true joint dislocation. Since the growth plate is still present, this is a Salter-Harris type I or II fracture. But the key here is that the injury is at the physis, leading to the displacement of the medial clavicle.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 22-year-old male with acute right sternoclavicular pain following a traumatic fall on an outstretched hand. The mechanism of injury (force transmitted along the clavicle) and localized tenderness suggest sternoclavicular joint (SCJ) involvement. Posterior sternoclavicular dislocations are rare but critical due to potential mediastinal structure compromise.

<step 2> Physical findings (posterior dip in the medial clavicle) align with posterior SCJ dislocation. However, radiographs were normal, likely due to overlapping bony structures obscuring the SCJ. This highlights the limitation of plain films in diagnosing posterior SCJ injuries.

<step 3> The 3D CT scan revealed posterior dislocation of the medial clavicle with a bony flake at the sternum. In young adults, the medial clavicular physis (growth plate) may remain open until ~25 years of age. A "flake" of bone attached to the sternum suggests a physeal avulsion injury rather than a pure ligamentous dislocation, as the epiphysis remains cartilaginous and poorly visualized on imaging.

<step 4> Failed closed reduction and intraoperative confirmation of physeal injury solidify the diagnosis. Posterior displacement in this age group often represents a physeal fracture-separation (Salter-Harris type I/II) rather than a true SCJ dislocation, as the weak physis fails before the robust sternoclavicular ligaments.

### Answer:
Posterior sternoclavicular physeal fracture-separation (medial clavicular physeal injury).

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a 22-year-old male with acute right sternoclavicular pain following a traumatic fall on an outstretched hand. The mechanism of injury (force transmitted along the clavicle) and localized tenderness suggest sternoclavicular joint (SCJ) involvement. Posterior sternoclavicular dislocations are rare but critical due to potential mediastinal structure compromise.

Physical findings (posterior dip in the medial clavicle) align with posterior SCJ dislocation. However, radiographs were normal, likely due to overlapping bony structures obscuring the SCJ. This highlights the limitation of plain films in diagnosing posterior SCJ injuries.

The 3D CT scan revealed posterior dislocation of the medial clavicle with a bony flake at the sternum. In young adults, the medial clavicular physis (growth plate) may remain open until ~25 years of age. A "flake" of bone attached to the sternum suggests a physeal avulsion injury rather than a pure ligamentous dislocation, as the epiphysis remains cartilaginous and poorly visualized on imaging.

Failed closed reduction and intraoperative confirmation of physeal injury solidify the diagnosis. Posterior displacement in this age group often represents a physeal fracture-separation (Salter-Harris type I/II) rather than a true SCJ dislocation, as the weak physis fails before the robust sternoclavicular ligaments.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial Diagnosis - Posterior Sternoclavicular Dislocation
- Clinical examination showed posterior dip and tenderness.
- CT scan confirmed posterior displacement of the medial clavicle.
- One CT section suggested possible physeal injury, prompting further evaluation.
Step 2: Revised Diagnosis - Medial Clavicular Physeal Injury
- Considered due to patient's age (22 years) and CT findings of a small epiphyseal bone.
- Confirmed intraoperatively with physeal separation and posterior locking of the clavicle.
Step 3: Exclusion of Pure Dislocation Without Physeal Injury
- Intraoperative findings confirmed physeal separation rather than a simple dislocation.
- Clinical presentation and imaging findings alone could not differentiate between dislocation and physeal injury.
- Intraoperative findings provided definitive confirmation of physeal separation.
Final Diagnosis: Medial clavicular physeal separation mimicking posterior sternoclavicular dislocation.
